heading: MODULO DICIOTTESIMO
card_summary: Chunk 136 of De Harmonia Mundi treats the nature and conditions of beatitude (felicità) across three concentus. Zorzi opens with definitions from Augustine and Plato (Philebus, Euthydemus), declaring that true happiness consists only in union with God, against Cicero's "freedom from all things" and grounded in Aristotle's Politica. The chapter then follows Origen's Contra Celsum in describing a graduated visionary ascent — ecstasy, rapture, paradise, third heaven — where the blessed learn the reasons behind the Levitical code, angelic hierarchies, stellar intelligences, medicinal virtues of plants, and providential causes of all events. Hermes Trismegistus (the Poimandres/Corpus Hermeticum) and the Wisdom of Solomon are marshalled alongside John's "unction" (1 John 2) and Plotinus to show that the divine Mind illuminates pure souls. The final section debates, via Origen and later against mainstream scholastic theology, whether beatified souls continue to grow in knowledge after death, citing Christ's own growth in wisdom (Luke 2) and the Pauline "solid food for the perfect" (Hebrews 5) as evidence for perpetual spiritual progress toward full unification with the infinite.
essay:
Now I'll expand this essay from ~1200 words to 2000-4000 words by adding deeper scholarly analysis, more detailed engagement with sources, and comprehensive explanations throughout. I'll preserve all blockquotes, citations, and formatting exactly as-is, and add substantive new paragraphs.

## Overview

Modulo Diciottesimo, the eighteenth module of the *Terzo Cantico* of Zorzi's *De Harmonia Mundi*, is organized around three *concentus* devoted entirely to *felicità* — beatitude, its definition, its mode of attainment, and to whom perfect happiness is finally granted. The module runs from p. 2723 to p. 2740 in Campanini's critical edition and is one of the most philosophically ambitious chapters in the third book, weaving together Augustinian theology, Platonic epistemology, Hermetic gnosis, Origenist eschatology, and Christian Kabbalah in a sustained argument about the soul's ultimate union with God.

The very structure of this module—three concentus devoted to a single theme—underscores the centrality of beatitude (*felicità*) to Zorzi's entire theological enterprise. In the *De Harmonia Mundi*, beatitude is not merely an eschatological doctrine relegated to a single section on the afterlife; rather, it functions as the telos (end) toward which the entire cosmic and human order is oriented. From the first concentus of the work onward, Zorzi has been building an argument that all creation—from the primordial emanation of being from the divine source, through the celestial hierarchies, through the sublunary realm of generation and corruption, to the human soul—tends toward a reunion with God. This eighteenth module thus stands as the philosophical and theological culmination of that argument, the point at which Zorzi's entire system achieves its eschatological resolution.

---

## Concentus I: Defining Beatitude — Augustine, Plato, Aristotle, and Cicero

Zorzi opens with a canonical comparison of definitions of happiness. Augustine's formula — *"beatus qui habet quae vult"* (blessed is he who has all he wills") — is set beside Plato's formulation in the *Philebus* ("blessed is he to whom nothing is lacking") and the *Euthydemus* ("happiness is when things happen according to our desires"). Zorzi draws these together: none of these conditions can be met except by one who adheres and is united to the Source of all goods (*Sorgente di tutti i beni*), from whom everything can be drawn at will.

The opening theological proposition is thus: *"Beato e felice, dunque, è chi è unito a Dio."* The seraphim of Isaiah 6 proclaiming "Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God of hosts; the whole earth is full of his glory" serve as the paradigm of creaturely participation in divine glory.

Zorzi's invocation of Augustine marks a deliberate move to ground his definition of beatitude in the Christian mystical-theological tradition rather than in purely philosophical speculation. Augustine's definition—that the blessed one is he who possesses all he desires (*quae vult*)—is deceptively simple. It rests on a deep insight: that human desire, when rightly ordered, is not satiated by finite goods or the accumulation of external possessions, but only by union with the infinite Source. Augustine's *Confessions* opens with the famous declaration, "Our heart is restless until it rests in you," a formulation that Zorzi invokes implicitly throughout the module. The soul that wants for nothing is the soul whose will has been entirely conformed to and subsumed within the divine Will. This is not, in Augustine's understanding, a negation of the will but rather its perfection and fulfillment—the will no longer rebels against itself because it wills only what God wills.

When Zorzi brings Plato into this conversation via the *Philebus* ("blessed is he to whom nothing is lacking," *ὦ μακάριος ᾧ μηδὲν ἐνδέεσθαι*), he is invoking one of Plato's most sustained philosophical treatments of pleasure (*ἡδονή*) and the good life (*εὐδαιμονία*). In the *Philebus*, Socrates and Protarchus debate whether pleasure or knowledge constitutes the supreme good, ultimately concluding that the good life is a mixture of pleasure and wisdom in which wisdom predominates. Plato's point—that the human who lacks nothing is happy—operates on both a negative and positive register: negatively, the happy person is not enslaved to bodily appetites or restless desire; positively, the happy person possesses knowledge of the Good itself and participates in it. Zorzi's synthesis here collapses the Platonic and Augustinian positions by arguing that they both point toward the same truth: that true happiness consists in possession of the infinite, and that this infinite Source can be possessed only through union (*unito*).

The appeal to Aristotle's *Politica* marks a further refinement. Aristotle's dictum — that God himself is the witness that happiness does not lie in external goods, since God is blessed and uses none — demolishes the Ciceronian claim that the happy life consists in freedom from all things (*vacatio rerum*). This Ciceronian position, familiar from the *De Officiis*, equates happiness with the absence of conflict and disturbance (*otium*), a state of ataraxia or apatheia achievable through withdrawal from civic affairs. But Aristotle's argument, as Zorzi frames it, shows that this negative conception of happiness is inadequate: God is supremely happy, yet God is supremely active (the *actus purus* of medieval Aristotelian metaphysics). Happiness therefore cannot consist in the mere absence of external goods or troubles, but in the exercise of proper activity—the contemplation (*theoria*) of the highest things. Zorzi describes the refutation of Cicero pungently: *"hic corruit Cicero suo concussus ariete"* (here Cicero falls, battered by his own battering-ram), suggesting that Cicero's own principles, when pushed to their logical conclusion, undermine his position.

This opening section establishes what might be called Zorzi's *method of convergence*: the idea that divergent philosophical and theological traditions, when properly understood, converge on the same truth about the human soul's ultimate end. This method was inherited from Ficino and Pico, but Zorzi deploys it with systematic rigor. He is not simply syncretically mixing authorities; rather, he is arguing that Augustine, Plato, and Aristotle all testify, from within their respective conceptual frameworks, to a single metaphysical and spiritual reality—that human happiness is union with the divine Source.

---

## Concentus II: The Path to God and the Visionary Ascent

The second concentus is the doctrinal and mystical center of the module. It takes up the Pauline text of Romans 1:20 — *"Invisibilia Dei per ea quae facta sunt a creatura mundi intellecta conspiciuntur"* — to establish that the route to God may legitimately pass through creatures, provided the soul never turns away from God in doing so.

This verse is decisive for Zorzi's entire theological epistemology. Paul's claim—that the invisible things of God are perceived through those things that are made, understood in the mind—encodes a Neoplatonic principle of emanation and return: creatures are outflowings of divine goodness, and therefore the soul that contemplates creatures with spiritual eyes does not turn away from God but rather sees God manifest in and through creation. This is the via affirmativa, the way of affirmation, as opposed to the via negativa or way of negation exemplified by the Pseudo-Dionysian theology. For Zorzi, creatures are not obstacles to divine union but paths toward it, provided the soul does not rest in creatures but presses through them toward the Creator.

Zorzi elaborates Augustine's tripartite visionary schema from the *De Genesi ad litteram*: the *visio meridionalis* (direct vision of God himself), the *visio matutina* (vision of creatures in God), and the *visio vespertina* (vision of God in creatures). Origen's *Contra Celsum*, Book II, is cited for a "retrograde order" of the same schema.

> *"Nunc igitur ipsius Dei oblectantur visione (quae meridionalis dicitur) nunc creaturas in ipso percipiunt visione matutina, nunc autem ipsum in creaturis visione vespertina conspiciunt, ut diffuse et aperte Augustinus super Genesim disserit."*
> <cite>De Harmonia Mundi, Cantico Terzo, Tono Ottavo, p. 2557 (Latin text)</cite>

Augustine's three visions represent three modalities of mystical perception, each more intimate and direct than the last. The *visio vespertina*, the "evening vision," corresponds to the soul's perception of God reflected in the order and beauty of created things—the stars, the forms of plants and animals, the harmonies of music and geometry. This is the vision available to the contemplative at the threshold of mystical knowledge, but still mediated by creatures. The *visio matutina*, the "morning vision," reverses this: here the soul sees creatures themselves—not as separate entities, but as they exist in the divine Mind, as intelligible forms or ideas eternally present in God's intellect. This corresponds to what Plotinus calls the vision of intelligibles in the realm of Nous, the divine Intellect. Finally, the *visio meridionalis*, the "noonday vision," is the soul's direct and unmediated intuition of God himself, face to face, without any intermediary—not even the forms or ideas, but God in his bare unity and infinity.

Origen's contribution, invoked here by Zorzi, is to show that these three visions can be understood in a "retrograde order," meaning that the soul ascends from the evening vision through the morning vision to the noon vision, but the entire ascent is already implicit in God's order. The logical order (God's perspective) is noon-morning-evening, the order of emanation; the soteriological order (the soul's perspective) is evening-morning-noon, the order of return. Origen's eschatology, particularly as developed in the *Contra Celsum*, emphasizes that souls do not reach their final state all at once, but through an extended process of illumination and purification—a teaching that, as we shall see, becomes crucial to Zorzi's defense of eschatological progress in the third concentus.

### The Ecstatic Ascent and the Encyclopaedia of Hidden Knowledge

Following Origen's schema, Zorzi describes what souls learn when liberated from the body — whether by ecstasy (*extasis*) or rapture (*raptus*). They are conducted to a place variously called *paradisus delitiarum*, the *third heaven*, or the *seni e petto dell'Archetipo* (the breast and bosom of the Archetype). There they are instructed in what they had previously grasped only through images and mirrors (1 Cor. 13:12 echoed throughout).

Zorzi's terminology here is deliberately multivalent. *Extasis* and *raptus*—ecstasy and rapture—are two classical mystical states, discussed extensively by Pseudo-Dionysius and the medieval mystical theologians. Ecstasy (*extasis*) is a standing-outside of oneself, a release from the ordinary operations of discursive reason; rapture (*raptus*) is an even more violent seizure by the divine, a state in which the soul is overwhelmed and absorbed into the divine light. The place to which the soul is conducted has multiple names, each with its own theological resonance. *Paradisus delitiarum* is Eden, the garden of delights, but also the paradise promised to the thief on the cross. The "third heaven" (2 Corinthians 12:2-4, where Paul was "caught up" and "heard inexpressible things") is the highest heaven, where God dwells. The "breast and bosom of the Archetype" (*seni e petto dell'Archetipo*) is Zorzi's language for the innermost sanctuary of the divine nature, the place of ultimate intimacy and union—echoing John 1:18, where the Logos is said to be "in the bosom of the Father."

The encyclopaedia of knowledge granted to the soul in this state is expansive and explicitly Kabbalistic-Mosaic in its structure:

- The celestial spheres and the reasons for the arrangement of heavenly bodies
- The intelligences (*intelligentiae*) presiding over each star, and from which region of the Archetype each draws its virtue
- The priestly and Levitical orders, the jubilees, sabbatical years, feasts, and fasts
- The reasons for the laws of leprosy, seminal flux, and bodily defects that bar priests from the altar (Leviticus 21)
- The angelic minds and separated intelligences, their virtues and potestates, benevolent and malign
- The hierarchy of souls, their origins, grades of perfection, measure of grace and gifts
- The diversity of animal species
- The miraculous virtues of roots and herbs, and their countervailing properties

This encyclopaedic vision represents the culmination of Zorzi's entire metaphysical and theological project. Throughout the *De Harmonia Mundi*, Zorzi has argued that the Torah, properly understood through Kabbalistic exegesis, encodes the entire structure of reality—both the celestial hierarchies and the terrestrial order of nature. The beatified soul's vision of all these things is thus fundamentally a vision of the Torah's hidden meaning (*sod*). The "reasons" (*rationes*) for the arrangements of the heavens, the virtues of herbs, the meanings of priestly regulations—these are not extraneous knowledge but rather the intelligible structure that underlies the sensible order. When Zorzi specifies that the soul learns "the reasons for the laws of leprosy, seminal flux, and bodily defects that bar priests from the altar," he is not being antiquarian; rather, he is affirming that these Levitical regulations are not arbitrary divine commands but revelations of cosmic principles. Leprosy, in Kabbalistic symbolism, corresponds to spiritual corruption; ritual purity corresponds to spiritual integrity; the priesthood corresponds to the highest orders of celestial intelligence.

This encyclopaedic vision draws directly on the *Wisdom of Solomon* 7:17-21, which Zorzi cites at length:

> *"Ipse mihi dedit eorum quae sunt scientiam veram, ut sciam dispositiones orbis terrarum, et virtutes elementorum, initium, consummationem, medietatem, et vicissitudinem temporum, anni cursus, stellarum dispositiones, naturas animalium, iram bestiarum, vim ventorum, cogitationes hominum, differentias virgultorum, virtutes radicum, et quaecumque sunt abscondita, et improvisa didici. Omnium enim artifex docuit me sapientia."*
> <cite>Wisdom of Solomon 7:17-21, as cited in De Harmonia Mundi, Cantico Terzo, p. 2562</cite>

The book of Wisdom (also called the Wisdom of Solomon, *Sapientia*) was understood in the Renaissance as one of the most profound texts of the Old Testament, a work in which Greek philosophical concepts (particularly Stoicism and Platonism) were integrated with Hebrew revelation. The passage Zorzi cites describes Solomon's acquisition of Wisdom (*Sophia*, identified with the divine Intellect or Logos), and what immediately follows from this acquisition: true knowledge of the natural world in all its dimensions—the "disposition" (*dispositiones*) of the terrestrial sphere, the virtues (*virtutes*) of the elements, the cycles and seasonality of time, the courses of stars, the natures of animals, the powers of winds, the thoughts of human beings, the diversity of plants, the virtues of roots, and "all things that are hidden and unseen." This Solomonic catalogue provides Zorzi with scriptural warrant for claiming that beatific knowledge encompasses universal natural philosophy.

The crucial theological move here is that this knowledge is not the beatified soul's *acquisition* through study or logical reasoning, but its *illumination* through direct participation in divine Wisdom. The Wisdom that infuses the beatified soul grants it an immediate, intuitive understanding of all the harmonic relationships embedded in creation. Where earthly philosophy must labor through discursive reasoning and argument, the soul in glory perceives truth directly, in the eternal mind of God. Zorzi thus resolves a classical tension in medieval theology: the question of whether the beatified soul remains engaged in *studium* (intellectual inquiry) or rests in *quiets* (stillness). His answer is that the beatified soul remains in a state of perpetual, fruitless intellectual activity—but this activity is not discursive labor; it is the effortless contemplation of divine truths that the soul perceives with an immediacy and clarity no earthly philosophy can achieve.

### Hermes Trismegistus and the Hermetic Mind (*Nous*)

Zorzi supports the Solomonic vision with Hermes Trismegistus, whose *Poimandres* (Corpus Hermeticum I) supplies the parallel doctrine of the divine Mind (*Nous*):

> *"Adsum ego mens, id est divinus splendor intellectum vestrum perlustrans, his qui boni, pii, puri, religiosi, sanctique sunt, praesentiaque mea fert illis opem, adeo ut statim cuncta dignoscant."*
> <cite>Hermes Trismegistus, Poimandres (as cited in De Harmonia Mundi, Cantico Terzo, p. 2562)</cite>

The *Poimandres* ("The Shepherd of Men"), the first treatise of the Corpus Hermeticum, presents itself as a visionary dialogue between the author Hermes Trismegistus ("Thrice-Great Hermes") and the divine Mind (*Nous*). The Mind appears to Hermes and reveals the structure of the cosmos: how the Intelligible World emanates from the supreme God, how the sensible world is formed through the mediation of Fate (*Heimarmene*), and how the human soul, trapped in bodily generation, can ascend through knowledge and virtue toward reunion with the divine. The passage Zorzi cites presents the divine Nous addressing those who are pure, pious, religious, and holy: the Nous itself—the divine splendor that illuminates human understanding—is present to them and grants them immediate knowledge of all things (*statim cuncta dignoscant*, "they immediately know all things").

The Hermetic *crater* (the divine vessel/bowl from which souls drink the mind) is equated with the soul rightly disposed to receive divine splendor. This crater motif is central to the *Poimandres*: the divine Nous sends down into the world a cup or vessel filled with divine consciousness, and those who drink from it are initiated into gnosis (divine knowledge). Zorzi then cites the Hermetic address to Tat (the son of Hermes Trismegistus, who figures as the interlocutor in several Hermetic treatises): those who have received the gift of Mind (*mens*) are counted immortal among mortals because they comprehend all things on earth, sea, and above the heavens — they rise to behold the Good itself, in which they see all things.

The inclusion of Hermetic texts in a Christian theological treatise might seem problematic to a modern reader schooled in the post-Enlightenment narrative that sees Hermetic philosophy as "pagan" and therefore antithetical to Christian truth. But for Zorzi, writing in the late Renaissance, the Hermetic corpus was understood as a parallel revelation to Scripture—a Greek pagan theology that nevertheless anticipates Christian doctrines. The Hermetic *Nous* is read as a prefiguration of the Christian Logos; the Hermetic teaching about the soul's ascent through knowledge is understood as confirming the Christian mystical theology of union with God. This syncretism was given powerful theoretical justification in the work of Marsilio Ficino (whom Zorzi knew or knew about), who argued that the Hermetic texts represented a divine wisdom given to humanity at its beginning, preserved through the ages in various forms (Egyptian wisdom, Platonism, etc.), and ultimately fulfilled in Christ. Zorzi inherits this hermeneutic strategy and deploys it systematically throughout the *De Harmonia Mundi*.

This Hermetic text is synchronized with 1 John 2:20,27 — the apostle's teaching about the *unctio* (anointing) from the Holy One that teaches all things. Zorzi interprets this "unction" in the language of the "more secret theologians" (*secretiores theologi*) as the *mors osculi* — the "death of the kiss," the mystical death of union that is the culmination of the soul's journey. Psalm 115 ("Precious in the sight of the Lord is the death of his saints") and John 12:24 (the grain of wheat that must die to bear fruit) and Paul's Colossians 3:3 ("You are dead, and your life is hidden with Christ") are all marshalled in support.

The phrase *mors osculi* (death of the kiss) is drawn from the mystical exegetical tradition of the Song of Songs (*Canticum Canticorum*). In this tradition, the bride's prayer "Let him kiss me with the kisses of his mouth" (Song 1:2) is interpreted as the soul's longing for union with God so intimate and so absorbing that it constitutes a kind of death—a death of the separate ego, a dissolution of the boundary between the self and the divine. This death is not annihilation but consummation; it is the perfection of the soul through union. The *unctio* (anointing) of 1 John is reread through this mystical lens: the "unction from the Holy One" is not merely an imparting of knowledge or grace, but a mystical contact so profound that it effects a transformation of the soul into God. When Zorzi links this to the deaths of the martyrs ("precious in the sight of the Lord is the death of his saints"), he is suggesting that the ultimate death of all souls—the loss of separate existence in union with God—is what the martyrs anticipate and prefigure in their final sacrifice.

### Plotinus and the Sunlike Soul

Zorzi closes the concentus with a citation from Plotinus (Enneads, likely I.6 or V.1) on the sunlike soul:

> *"Qui puri sunt, habitantes secum, nihil impedimenti habentes, quo minus unum efficiantur, sed effecti divini, pulchri, atque Deiformes, tunc ascendunt in mentem ubi pulchras omnes species contemplantur, atque cognoscunt illas ideas esse, quae veras rerum indicant essentias, et quidditates."*
> <cite>Plotinus (as cited in De Harmonia Mundi, Cantico Terzo, p. 2563)</cite>

This passage—though attributed to Plotinus by Zorzi, and likely translated from a Latin Renaissance summary of the Enneads—encodes the entire Neoplatonic vision of the ascent of the soul. The souls that are pure, dwelling at peace with themselves, having no impediment to becoming one with the divine, become themselves divine, beautiful, and deiform (divine in form or nature). They then ascend into Nous (the divine Intellect), where they contemplate all beautiful forms and recognize these as the eternal ideas that reveal the true essences and quiddities of things.

The cosmological and mystical architecture here is the standard Plotinian schema: the One (the supreme, transcendent principle beyond being), Nous (the divine Intellect in which all forms or ideas exist eternally), Soul (the intermediary principle that generates and sustains the material cosmos), and Matter (the lowest principle, the substrate of generation). The soul's journey back to its source is a journey from Matter, through the material realm, through the celestial realms, through the Logos or cosmic order, and finally into Nous, where it beholds all things eternally present in their intelligible reality. The claim that the pure soul becomes "divine, beautiful, and deiform" (*divini, pulchri, atque Deiformes*) encodes the mystical axiom that union with God effects a deification (*theosis*) of the soul—not in the sense that the soul becomes God by substance, but in the sense that it participates in divine nature and is transformed by its contact with infinite goodness and beauty.

Zorzi's deployment of Plotinus at the climax of the second concentus is not peripheral but essential. The Neoplatonic framework provides him with a metaphysical vocabulary adequate to the mystical experiences he is trying to describe. Unlike some medieval theologians who viewed Neoplatonism with suspicion as a pagan philosophy incompatible with Christian revelation, Zorzi follows Ficino in reading Plotinus as a profound explorer of mystical union who, though not Christian, nevertheless achieved genuine philosophical insight into the nature of the soul's ascent toward the divine. The fact that Plotinus predated Christianity, in this view, does not invalidate his insights; rather, it confirms that the mystical path is inscribed in the very nature of the soul and can be accessed through philosophical contemplation as much as through Christian faith.

---

## Concentus III: To Whom Is Perfect Happiness Given — The Question of Eschatological Progress

The third concentus addresses a precise and contested eschatological question: does the beatified soul continue to grow in knowledge and union after death, or does it reach its destined measure instantaneously upon entry into glory?

This question may seem abstract and obscure to a modern reader, but it was at the heart of one of the most contentious theological debates of the late medieval and Renaissance periods. The mainstream position in Thomistic scholasticism held that upon the moment of death (for the elect), the soul is seized by the beatific vision in its fullness. The soul then rests in the eternal, unchanging possession of God, its understanding of God fixed in a single, eternal act of intuition. This position was grounded in a particular reading of medieval Aristotelian metaphysics: the soul's ultimate end is the vision of God face to face; once this end is achieved, all development or growth ceases. Growth (*progressus*) is a characteristic of things in potency moving toward actuality; the beatified soul has achieved full actuality and therefore can neither grow nor progress.

Zorzi, however, sides with Origen and other theological voices that challenge this position. His argument, developed across the remainder of the third concentus, is that growth and progress need not be understood as a movement from potency to actuality in the Aristotelian sense. Rather, the soul in glory can experience an infinite deepening of its knowledge and love of God precisely because God is infinite. The soul never exhausts the depths of the divine, never reaches a point at which there is nothing more to know or love. Growth in this context is not a defect or a sign of incompleteness; rather, it is the appropriate response of a finite creature to the infinite divine reality.

Zorzi presents this as a debate between *novitiores theologi* (the mainstream scholastic position: the soul reaches its fixed measure at once) and Origen together with unnamed *alii sapientes* (who hold that the blessed continue to grow). Zorzi clearly favors the Origenist position, devoting extensive space to a long excerpt from *Contra Celsum* in which Origen argues that the rational nature grows through each thing it learns in heaven — not as in fleshly life, but by intellectual increments — and that the Mind, once initiated, is conducted to perfect science, always seeing causes face to face.

> *"Cum ad coelestia loca pervenerint sancti, tunc amplius et perfectius cognoscent quam in peregrinatione fecerint. In qua cognitione crescet per singula rationalis natura... mente et sensu aucta ad perfectam scientiam mens iam initiata perducitur, nequaquam in posterum istis carnalibus sensibus praepedienda, sed intellectualibus incrementis aucta, semper omni ammoto velamine, et ad purum (ut ita dixerim) facie ad faciem rerum causas conspiciet."*
> <cite>Origen, Contra Celsum, Book II, as cited in De Harmonia Mundi, Cantico Terzo, pp. 2566-2567</cite>

Origen's reasoning here operates on two registers. First, he argues from the nature of the human soul: the rational soul is by nature apt to grow and to learn. This capacity for growth is constitutive of rationality itself. Origen then refines this: he distinguishes between the kind of growth that the soul experiences in the mortal body—a growth entangled with sensory perception, memory, and discursive reasoning—and the kind of growth the soul experiences in heaven. In the heavenly state, the soul is no longer impeded by bodily sensation. Yet it still grows, but now through "intellectual increments" (*intellectualibus incrementis*)—through an ever-deepening apprehension of intelligible truths.

The analogy Origen (and Zorzi) employ is nutritive: just as the body is nourished to growth in youth and then to sustenance in maturity, so the mind in glory continues to feed on *theoremata* and *intellectus rerum* — contemplations and intellections of causes — as proper food. Food sustains the body; knowledge sustains the soul. The blessed soul therefore perpetually "feeds" on the contemplation of divine causes, drawing ever-greater depth of understanding from this engagement.

Origen's position here is informed by his doctrine of the *aionios* (the eternal, the age to come). For Origen, the coming age is not a static eternity but an endless development. Time itself may cease in the conventional sense, but change, growth, and deepening understanding do not cease; rather, they are freed from the constraints of bodily temporality and the vicissitudes of moral striving. The soul's ascent continues infinitely, a perpetual approach toward God that never reaches a point of completion.

### Christ's Growth in Wisdom and the Exegetical Argument

Zorzi then makes a bold exegetical move, citing Luke 2:40 and 2:52 — *"Puer autem crescebat et confortabatur plenus sapientia"* (And the child grew and became strong, full of wisdom) and *"Iesus proficiebat sapientia, aetate, et gratia apud Deum et homines"* (Jesus advanced in wisdom, age, and grace before God and men) — to argue that even the God-man Jesus grew in wisdom, not merely in experience or in appearance. To explain this growth as merely apparent would be to accuse Scripture of lying. Therefore, the human Jesus, united to God, truly grew in merit and wisdom up to the fullness the Father had preordained. This becomes the model for the just generally growing toward the *visio meridionalis* — Augustine's noon-vision of God face to face.

This exegetical argument is daringly christological. In medieval and Renaissance theology, the question of Christ's knowledge was perennially vexed. If Christ is the incarnate God, does he possess the divine omniscience? If so, how could he grow in wisdom? The standard scholastic solution, following Thomas Aquinas, posited that Christ possessed full divine knowledge while incarnate, but that his human soul underwent a kind of progressive manifestation or revelation of knowledge—he knew all things from the moment of conception, but he learned and grew in the mode of human learning. This allowed scholastics to affirm both Christ's divine omniscience and the biblical narratives of his learning and growth.

Zorzi's strategy is more daring: he takes the Lukan statements at face value and argues that the human Jesus truly grew. This human growth is compatible with divine union because growth itself is not a defect when the goal of growth is infinite. The God-man grows in wisdom precisely because he is united to God, and this growth in wisdom models the growth that the saint or blessed soul experiences. The implication is that the beatified soul, in its own degree, participates in the same pattern as Christ: perpetual advancement in union and understanding.

Paul's Ephesians 4:13 is cited — *"Donec occurramus omnes in unitate fidei... in virum perfectum, in mensuram aetatis plenitudinis Christi"* (until we all attain to the unity of the faith and of the knowledge of the Son of God, to mature manhood, to the measure of the stature of the fullness of Christ) — as apostolic confirmation that ecclesial and eschatological growth is toward a perfect measure that is not yet instantaneously given. The church, the body of Christ, is growing toward a measure (*mensuram*) of maturity (*plenitudinem*) that it has not yet achieved. If the church collective is in process of growth toward fuller maturity, it stands to reason that the individual soul continues to grow as well.

### The Purgatorial Preparation and Plotinian Purification

Zorzi acknowledges the mainstream scholastic position with characteristic diplomatic hedging — *"nos cum ecclesia et cum Christo sumus hic et ubique"* (we are with the church and with Christ here and everywhere) — but presses on to note that souls not yet fully purified at death require a place of purification (*locus in quo expurgentur*) before they can withstand the full light without being overwhelmed. This is the Plotinian eye-metaphor again: the impure or weak eye is blinded by the most radiant light; it must first be made solar before it can bear the solar vision.

Here Zorzi invokes the doctrine of purgatory—a doctrine that became increasingly important in medieval theology and was a flashpoint of the Reformation debates. For Zorzi, however, purgatory is not punitive in the first instance; it is purificatory and preparatory. Souls that reach the threshold of beatitude but are not yet fully purified must undergo a process of perfection before they can endure the presence of God. This process is not merely the cleansing of past sins but the positive transformation of the soul into a state of receptivity adequate to receive the divine light.

Zorzi's language of the soul being "made solar" (*effici solarem*) directly echoes Plotinus's teaching that "the eye must become solar to see the sun." Just as the physical eye, if weak or diseased, cannot endure the radiance of the sun without being damaged, so the soul that is not yet purified and perfected cannot endure the radiance of divine glory. The process of purgation is thus the soul's preparation and transformation so that it becomes capable of receiving and bearing the divine illumination. This is why purgatory is not a punishment inflicted from outside but a necessary process of transformation—necessary because the soul, even if it dies in grace, may not yet be in the state of perfection requisite for the beatific vision.

Solomon's Proverbs 4:18 — *"Semita iustorum sicut lux splendens vadens et lucens usque ad perfectum diem"* (The path of the just is like a shining light, going and shining until the perfect day) — seals the argument: the path of the just is a light that increases until it is the full day of the beatific vision. The metaphor is explicitly one of growth: the light does not appear fully formed but rather grows and increases until it becomes the perfect day. The "perfect day" is the full beatific vision, but the image makes clear that the journey toward this vision is not instantaneous; it is a gradual brightening and intensification.

The module closes with the angels of 1 Peter 1:12 who still *desire* to look into God — a desire that cannot be vain lest it cause suffering to the blessed, which would contradict the nature of beatitude. Whether by perpetual growth or by an already-fixed terminal measure, the conclusion is the same: *"felicitas perfecta datur his qui iam devenerunt ad extremam metam sui progressus."* (Perfect happiness is given to those who have reached the final measure of their progress.)

This final formulation is characteristic of Zorzi's diplomatic style: he frames the conclusion in language that does not wholly commit him to either the Origenist or the scholastic position. But the weight of his argumentation throughout the concentus has clearly favored the Origenist view that the soul continues to grow and progress even in the beatific state.

---

## Structural and Thematic Significance

This module synthesizes the principal strands of Zorzi's Christian Kabbalah as it reaches toward its eschatological conclusion:

1. **Encyclopaedic natural philosophy as beatific knowledge**: The detailed catalogue of what souls learn in paradise — from astral intelligences to Levitical purity laws to medicinal plants — is not decorative. It reflects Zorzi's conviction that the Torah encodes the entire rational order of creation, and that full understanding of Scripture coincides with full knowledge of nature. The Wisdom of Solomon citation is the pivot. Zorzi's vision of the beatified soul's knowledge is thus fundamentally holistic: the soul knows all things because all things—whether celestial or terrestrial, animate or inanimate—reflect the divine order encoded in Scripture and in the cosmic intelligibility that emanates from God.

2. **Hermeticism as confirmatory tradition**: The *Poimandres* and the Tat dialogues are cited not as alternatives to Christian revelation but as pagan anticipations of the same truth — the soul's assimilation to the divine Mind. This syncretism is characteristic of the Ficinian-Pico tradition Zorzi inherits. The Hermetic texts serve as independent witnesses to truths that Christianity proclaims more perfectly and directly; they legitimate the philosophical investigation of the soul's ascent by showing that this investigation has ancient and honorable pedigree.

3. **Origenism**: The module's most daring theological move is its sustained defense of eschatological progress against the Thomistic position of terminal beatific measure. Zorzi's alignment with Origen, qualified by his profession of ecclesial orthodoxy, is a recurring feature of the *De Harmonia Mundi* and connects it to the broader Renaissance rehabilitation of Origen. In many ways, Zorzi's entire theological project is an Origenist project: a theology of cosmic restoration and return, of the redemption and perfection of all things through their reintegration with God.

4. **Kabbalistic structure underlying the Levitical excursus**: The enumeration of priestly orders, jubilees, sabbatical years, leprosy laws, and bodily defects barring altar access is a structural replay of the Kabbalistic reading of the Torah as a theosophical map. The blessed soul in heaven does not merely know these laws as laws but understands their hidden cosmological and spiritual rationale. Each mitzvah (commandment) is a vehicle for the transmission of divine wisdom; each ritual is a mirror of celestial realities. The soul's vision of this hidden Torah—the Torah as it exists in the mind of God, not merely as written on parchment—is the capstone of Zorzi's entire epistemological vision.

5. **The *mors osculi* and mystical death**: The identification of the "unction" of 1 John with the "death of the kiss" (*mors osculi*) links the module to the tradition of mystical exegesis of the Song of Songs, in which death-by-divine-kiss is the consummation of the soul's union with God. This term appears without further elaboration, suggesting it is a technical term familiar to Zorzi's intended readership among "secretiores theologi." The phrase encapsulates the paradoxical nature of mystical union: the soul achieves its perfection precisely through a kind of death, a cessation of its separate identity in absorption into the divine. This death is the ultimate beatitude, the ultimate life.
